prompts.label: Choose a first language
prompts.text:
I'm new to programming. Help me pick a first language based on my goal: web front-end, web back-end, data/ML, mobile, or systems.

- Compare 2–3 realistic options per goal
- Mention learning curve and job market briefly
- Suggest a tiny first project for the top pick

No gatekeeping — practical advice only.

prompts.label: Compiled vs interpreted
prompts.text:
Explain compiled vs interpreted languages with examples (C/Rust vs Python/JavaScript).

- What happens at run time vs build time
- JIT as a middle ground (Java, JS engines)
- When performance differences actually matter for beginners

Use a simple diagram in ASCII or bullets.

prompts.label: Static vs dynamic typing
prompts.text:
Compare static typing (TypeScript, Java, Rust) vs dynamic typing (Python, JavaScript).

- When type errors are caught
- Pros: IDE help, refactoring safety
- Cons: verbosity, learning curve

Give one bug example that static types catch early.

prompts.label: OOP vs functional style
prompts.text:
Explain OOP vs functional programming styles without fanboy wars.

- Classes/objects vs pure functions and immutability
- Languages that blend both (Python, JS, C#)
- When OOP helps modeling and when functions simplify pipelines

3 bullets each side, 2 hybrid tips.

prompts.label: Memory management models
prompts.text:
Compare garbage collection, manual malloc/free, and ownership/borrowing (Rust).

- One-sentence how each works
- Common failure modes (leaks, use-after-free, data races teaser)
- Which languages use which model

Beginner-friendly, no 20-page essay.

prompts.label: Polyglot project boundaries
prompts.text:
When is it reasonable to use multiple languages in one product?

- Typical split: UI in TS, API in Python/Go, hot path in Rust/C++
- Costs: tooling, hiring, deployment complexity
- Anti-pattern: random language per microservice without reason

Decision checklist in bullets.

prompts.label: Learn a second language faster
prompts.text:
I know one language well and want to learn another. Give a 2-week study plan.

- Map familiar concepts (variables, loops, modules, tests)
- Focus on what's different: package manager, types, concurrency, idioms
- One small porting exercise: rewrite a 50-line script

Assume I already know Python or JavaScript — ask which if unclear.

prompts.label: Read unfamiliar language code
prompts.text:
Teach how to read a codebase in a language I barely know.

- Start from entry point, tests, and README
- Ignore syntax sugar at first; track data flow
- Use LSP/go-to-definition and grep
- Build a glossary of 10 keywords in that language

Example workflow on a tiny open-source repo.

prompts.label: FFI and calling native code
prompts.text:
Explain FFI at a high level: why Python/Rust/Go call C libraries.

- C ABI as common denominator
- ctypes / cffi / pyo3 / cgo — names only, one line each
- Safety and ABI stability warnings

When to rewrite in native code vs stay in high-level language.

prompts.label: Standard library vs ecosystem
prompts.text:
Compare languages with rich stdlibs vs thin core + huge package repos.

- Examples: Go stdlib, Python batteries, npm/crates.io scale
- Supply chain and dependency audit basics
- Pin versions and lockfiles — why they matter

5 practical rules for picking third-party libs.

prompts.label: Package managers compared
prompts.text:
Compare package managers: npm/yarn/pnpm, pip/uv/poetry, cargo, go modules, Maven/Gradle.

- Install, lockfile, publish workflow in one table (markdown)
- Virtual environments vs global installs
- Common beginner mistakes (sudo pip install)

No deep config — orientation map.

prompts.label: Concurrency models overview
prompts.text:
Survey concurrency approaches: OS threads, async/await, goroutines, actors, CSP.

- Which languages favor which model
- GIL teaser for Python in one sentence
- Rule of thumb: I/O-bound vs CPU-bound

Point to where to learn more in Python asyncio vs Go vs Rust tokio.

prompts.label: Benchmarks and when they lie
prompts.text:
Explain language benchmarks (Benchmarks Game, hello-world myths).

- Micro-benchmark pitfalls
- Real bottlenecks: I/O, DB, network, algorithm choice
- Profile before rewriting in another language

Give a sane decision flowchart in bullets.

prompts.label: Migrate or rewrite legacy stack
prompts.text:
We have a legacy monolith in language X. Outline migration options to language Y.

- Strangler fig pattern, incremental modules
- Rewrite risks and cost estimation factors
- When NOT to migrate (it works, team skill)

Bullet roadmap, no silver bullet.

prompts.label: Scripting vs systems languages
prompts.text:
Define scripting/glue languages vs systems languages with examples.

- Shell, Python, Lua for automation
- C, C++, Rust, Zig for kernels, games, embedded
- Hybrid: Python orchestrating Rust extension

Help me label my current task which bucket it fits.

prompts.label: TypeScript as JavaScript superset
prompts.text:
Why TypeScript exists on top of JavaScript for teams.

- Types at compile time, erase to JS
- any vs unknown, strict mode benefits
- When plain JS is still fine

Tiny example: interface User and a function using it.

prompts.label: REPL and exploratory coding
prompts.text:
Teach REPL-driven development for learning APIs.

- Python REPL, IPython, Node, Ruby irb — pick one and demo
- When REPL beats editing files
- Saving experiments into proper modules

5 commands to try in a REPL session.

prompts.label: Idioms and readable style
prompts.text:
What does idiomatic code mean in a language?

- Examples: list comprehension vs loop, Option vs null, with vs try/finally
- Linters and formatters (ruff, rustfmt, prettier)
- Reading style guides for 2 languages side by side

Encourage consistency over clever one-liners.

prompts.label: Domain-specific languages (DSL)
prompts.text:
Introduce DSLs: SQL, regex, HTML/CSS, Terraform, Makefile.

- When a DSL beats a general-purpose language
- Risks: learning many mini-languages, debugging
- Building internal DSLs — only mention, not full tutorial

List 5 DSLs I likely already touch as a developer.

prompts.label: Breadth vs depth in career
prompts.text:
Guide on balancing language breadth vs depth for a developer career.

- T-shaped: one primary stack, awareness of others
- When to specialize (embedded, data, frontend)
- Learning plan: master one, skim two, revisit yearly

No code — mentorship tone, 8 bullets max.
